coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots for food contaminants detection applications
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Jothi Vinoth
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Analyzing food composition and detecting contaminants is important for monitoring food quality and assessing public health risks. Traditional food analysis methods can meet laboratories' routine food analysis needs, but critical food safety situations require rapid, time-saving, and low-cost analytical technologies, including on-site, portable, and convenient test kits. As one of these methods, fluorescence analysis has great potential in food safety due to its outstanding features such as high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost, and fast result output. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are innovative 0D carbon-based nanoparticles that have recently emerged as promising replacements for standard fluorescent materials. CQDs have many advantages over conventional fluorescent materials such as quantum dots, organic dyes, and metal nanoclusters, including easy preparation, non-blinking, low cytotoxicity, low cost, high resistance to photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility. As a result, CQD-based fluorescence detection provides a useful analytical tool for analyzing food-related analytes. This review provides an overview of the state-of-the-art synthesis methods, CQD fluorescence properties, detection process, and design ideas for CQD-based fluorescence detectors for food analysis. The advantages and disadvantages of CQD-based fluorescence sensors for detecting food contaminants are also highlighted.

Produced by the esterification of phosphoric acid and alcohol. Contain elemental K, N, and P. Inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity. Neurotoxicity.

Disorders such as Alzheimer’s and dementia. Neurotoxic effects on developing organisms. Impaired memory and concentration, disorientation, severe depression, irritability, confusion, headache, speech difficulties, delayed reaction times, nightmares, sleepwalking, drowsiness, or insomnia
Contain sulfur. Foul odors with sweet-smelling organosulfur derivatives (like saccharin).

Axonic excitotoxins. They are classified based on their mechanism of biological action, as they do not share a common chemical structure.
Toxic to aquatic organisms but generally harmless to humans. Poisoning in cats can result in seizures, fever, ataxia and even death.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots for food contaminants detection applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Analyzing food composition and detecting contaminants is important for monitoring food quality and assessing public health risks. Traditional food analysis methods can meet laboratories' routine food analysis needs, but critical food safety situations require rapid, time-saving, and low-cost analytical technologies, including on-site, portable, and convenient test kits. As one of these methods, fluorescence analysis has great potential in food safety due to its outstanding features such as high sensitivity, simple operation, low cost, and fast result output. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are innovative 0D carbon-based nanoparticles that have recently emerged as promising replacements for standard fluorescent materials. CQDs have many advantages over conventional fluorescent materials such as quantum dots, organic dyes, and metal nanoclusters, including easy preparation, non-blinking, low cytotoxicity, low cost, high resistance to photo-bleaching, and excellent biocompatibility. As a result, CQD-based fluorescence detection provides a useful analytical tool for analyzing food-related analytes. This review provides an overview of the state-of-the-art synthesis methods, CQD fluorescence properties, detection process, and design ideas for CQD-based fluorescence detectors for food analysis. The advantages and disadvantages of CQD-based fluorescence sensors for detecting food contaminants are also highlighted.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Control of toxic substances and contaminants in food has become a global issue in food safety. Food toxins can come from various sources, including the environment and microbial contamination, and can be organic or inorganic [1]. Some serious contaminants include copper, lead, iron, dioxins, mercuric fluoride, bisphenols, food colors, polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs), pesticides, herbicides, fertilizers, animal growth hormones, and preservatives [2]. Several pharmaceuticals commonly used in humans and animals can contaminate water by leaching or migrating through food packaging materials [3]. Their toxicity depends on the individual's health, age, dose, frequency of exposure, and sensitivity [4]. Food contamination can occur throughout the food supply chain, including food manufacturing and distribution processes, and it is well known that food contamination hurts human health and economic development. There is evidence that environmental contaminants have a serious impact on food safety. Various contaminants can be found in the air, water, and soil, which can migrate to crops and food animals, as shown in Fig. 1 [5]. Traces of contaminants in the food chain can cause certain health problems. Therefore, many administrative agencies can detect food contamination and set maximum residue levels (EC, 2006; EC, 2010; MOA, 2002) [6].

Currently, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), gas chromatography (GC), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS), and other advanced equipment are widely used to accurately and quantitatively detect food ingredients or contaminants in food [7]. However, this analysis method requires expensive equipment, skilled operators, and much analysis time, including extensive and rigorous sample preparation and cost [8,11]. Therefore, methods capable of high-speed detection, such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), fluorescence (FL), and electrochemical sensors (ES), have been proposed. In particular, nanotechnology-based fluorescence sensors offer many advantages over existing laboratory methods to become a useful tool for detecting environmental toxins in food [9,10]. Researchers have recently used fluorescence detection or other detection methods due to several advantages such as fast sample preparation, time-saving, low detection limit, accurate readout, less human resources, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and convenience [12]. Accordingly, the fluorescent sensor approach can accurately detect harmful pesticides, biomolecules, metal ions, and food additives, and recently, the carbon-based nanomaterial fluorescent sensor platform has received much attention [13].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new carbon nanomaterial discovered accidentally by Xu et al. in 2004 while separating carbon nanotubes. They are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm [14]. CQDs, an eco-friendly carbon nanomaterial, exhibit stable physical and chemical properties, strong biocompatibility, moisture diffusion ability, and ease of manufacturing. CQDs with quasi-spherical structures have attracted the attention of researchers due to their excellent photophysical properties, including strong fluorescence properties, tunable size, excellent ultraviolet (UV) absorption, and photobleaching resistance [15,16]. The CQDs are classified into graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer dots (PDs) based on the core structure of carbon [17]. All types of CQDs have small particle sizes and large specific surface areas. Surface atoms are active and combine with other atoms or chemical groups to achieve various purposes [18]. In addition, CQDs have superior optical properties compared to existing semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and organic fluorescent dyes and reduce the negative impact on the environment by reducing the harmful metallic elements in the manufacturing of green composites [19–21].

Various CQDs have been produced based on various sources and processes for various applications in various fields, such as the chemical industry, food, medicine, and environment. Although several reviews have been published on CQDs synthesis, properties and applications in general, a detailed review of their applications in analyzing food contaminants is not yet available, perhaps due to the difficulties in studying impurities in highly complex nutritional matrices. This review briefly describes CQDs preparation and fluorescence properties and CQDs-based sensors' fluorescence detection method, and design principles are explained in detail. Finally, current issues and emerging trends in CQDs sensor applications for food contaminants analysis have been discussed.

Carbon quantum dots can be prepared using a variety of carbon-containing compounds, especially natural materials and industrial wastes containing abundant phytochemicals, as stable and sustainable carbon sources for CQDs [30]. Agricultural and food processing wastes and industrial by-products provide sufficient carbon to drive CQD production and have great potential for expanded practical applications. Vegetable and animal wastes contain high amino, sulfur, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups [31]. In carbon quantum dots, the functional groups remain at the boundary due to the synthesis method and materials used. These functional groups provide tunable surface functionalization to CQDs. Although CQDs synthesized from chemical precursors have the same properties, other factors such as preparation drawbacks, raising environmental concerns, and production costs have led to a focus on natural resources in CQD manufacturing. Using biowaste as a source for CQDs synthesis also includes avenues for more sustainable waste management strategies. Carbonaceous precursors could only be used for CQD synthesis, resulting in CQDs with low quantum yield and solubility. Many carbon sources have been investigated to produce simple, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly materials with unique optical and electrical properties using various synthesis techniques. The choice of starting material generally affects the carbon core structure of the final CQDs, and most of these techniques have improved in terms of the obtained quantum yield. As a result, this class also makes cases where quantum yields are obtained using modified precursors. Surface passivation of synthesized CQDs can increase quantum yield and solubility [32].

By-products derived from crustacean shells, human hair, animal skin, milk, and animal manure have been used as raw materials for CQDs manufacturing [33]. Elango et al. recently reported the fabrication of highly fluorescent CQDs using crab and shrimp shells [34]. These CQDs were synthesized from animal waste using a hydrothermal process for feasibility and environmental friendliness. Das et al. prepared CQDs with high quantum yields using pigeon wings/eggs (25%) and crab shells (20%) [35]. CQDs were synthesized using various plant wastes and ingredients, including orange peel, lemon peel, watermelon peel, neem leaf, banana peel, mango peel, coffee grounds, and curcumin. Plant-based materials generally showed higher quantum yields (40%) than animal waste and industrial by-products [36].

CQDs made from industrial by-products can be less polluting, and the main advantage of this process is that the materials can be reused. Major industrial wastes include paper mills, plastic waste, animal feed, and petroleum [37]. The hydrothermal approach using various reaction conditions provided high quantum yields for cat feedstock (28%) and petroleum coke (22%) [38]. Petroleum coke, on the other hand, is a promising material that exhibits sufficient quantum yield at high temperatures and in a much shorter time [39].
CQDs can generally be synthesized by “top-down” and “bottom-up” routes ( Fig. 2), which can be further classified in several ways.

Top-down processes use chemical or physical techniques to break down large carbonaceous materials or split them into smaller CQDs. Many modified carbon precursors, such as graphite and candle soot, have been used to fabricate CQDs [22]. This approach reduces the size of bulk material to the desired CQD size through electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, chemical vapor deposition, chemical oxidation, and molecular beam pathology. Top-down techniques for CQD synthesis are effective but often operate under acidic, high-potential, and high-energy conditions, making reaction conditions difficult to control [23].

In the arc discharge process reactor, carbon molecules rearrange during decomposition in a large carbon source driven by a gas plasma generated within a closed reactor at the anode electrode, as shown in Fig. 3 [40]. When an electric current flows, the reactor's temperature reaches 4000 K and plasma with enormous energy is generated. Carbon vapor condenses within the cathode to form CQDs. An American research team synthesized single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) using the arc discharge method and created three types of carbon nanoparticles with similar molecular weights and photoluminescence [41]. The fluorescence at 365 nm of CQDs prepared this way is orange, cyan, or yellow. HNO3 was used to insert carboxyl groups (hydrophilic) into the CQD surface due to the size and limited yield of carbon particles generated during the arc discharge process. CQDs synthesized using this method had high water solubility and large particle sizes, typically around 18 nm. Additionally, arc discharge nanoparticles may contain many complex components that are difficult to filter. Chao-Mujica et al. produced CQDs from aqueous components using a submerged arc discharge [42]. The prepared CQDs emitted two coherent bands with a quantum yield of 16% and spectra of 320–340 nm (band A) and 400–410 nm (band B) at excitation wavelengths of 275 nm (band A) and 285 nm, respectively [42]. These CQDs were fluorescent biomarkers in cell culture experiments on mouse L929 fibroblasts. This method can be used to generate CQDs for use in bioimaging applications. This type of CQD has a small particle size, low quantum yield (QY), and good photoluminescence (PL) properties [43].

Sun et al. used laser ablation of a carbon target in an argon gas stream (through a water bubbler) transporting water vapor at 900 °C and 75 kPa using a Q-switched Nd: YAG laser (10 Hz, 1064 nm) [44]. Refluxing in nitric acid for 12 h and surface passivation with simple organic molecules such as PEG1500N (amine-terminated polyethylene glycol) or poly(propionyl ethyleneimine-ethyleneimine) (PPEI-EI) produces bright luminescence. Fluorescent CQDs were created by irradiating a suspension of carbon compounds in an organic solvent with a laser. The surface properties of CQDs can be modified by choosing an organic solvent that produces tunable luminescence [45]. For the experimental control, the origin of luminescence was linked to the surface state of the ligand localized on his CQD surface. Lee et al. [46] synthesized CQDs using nanocarbon materials and liquid solvents as precursor materials. A conventional method dispersed 0.02 g of the carbon material in 50 mL of solvent (acetone, ethanol, water). After sonication, 4 mL of the solution was placed in a glass cell and irradiated with a pulsed laser with a second harmonic wavelength of 532 nm. The solution was then centrifuged to obtain a supernatant containing CQDs [47]. A simple method to fabricate CQDs via laser irradiation of carbon precursors dispersed in several standard organic solvents is shown in Fig. 4.

The electrochemical method is a process that non-selectively chemically cuts materials such as carbon nanotubes, graphite, and carbon fiber electrodes [49]. CQDs are synthesized by synthesizing electrocatalysts using a single-pot electrochemical method. CQDs were created using a three-electrode configuration, with a working electrode (WE) made of graphite, a counter electrode (CE) made of platinum foil or Pt wire, and a reference electrode (RE), as shown in Fig. 5. Colorless CQDs dispersion was achieved by providing a constant potential of 5 V to the graphite electrode (working electrode) for 3 h in a nitrogen (N) environment using an alkaline solution such as 0.1 M NaOH/EtOH as electrolyte [50].

Electrochemical approaches produce CQDs of heterogeneous sizes, requiring further purification through filtration or chromatography to produce monodisperse CQDs [51]. Electrolysis of the solution produces NaCl, H2, and Cl2 at the cathode and anode, which play a role in forming CQDs. Deng et al. prepared CQDs using a one-pot electrochemical technique and fabricated them using alcohol via a three-electrode device [52]. They used platinum (Pt) sheets as working and counter electrodes. A calomel electrode attached to the drag capillary was used as a reference electrode. A rubber stopper was used to adjust the distance between electrodes. As shown in Fig. 5, the electrochemical carbonization of alcohols into very small particles is a simple and successful process for producing high-quality CQDs.

Bottom-up methods mainly involve large-scale production of CQDs by carbonization and polymerization methods [24]. Bottom-up methods to fabricate CQDs include solvothermal [25], microwave-assisted [26], combustion [27], hydrothermal [17], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [28], and ultrasonic-assisted methods [29]. The advantage of a bottom-up strategy is that you have complete control over the size and path of your product. The bottom-up approach is simpler, easier and greener, allowing for faster mass production at lower costs. Additionally, most renewable resources contain small biomolecules that can be carbonized to produce green CQDs.

Hydrothermal methods are the most widely used because they allow rapid heating of aqueous solutions containing the required precursors. Various small molecules, such as amino acids [53] and citric acid [54], have previously been used as CQD precursors. However, the synthesis of these small compounds is complicated by high cost and loss of resources. In bottom-up hydrothermal treatment methods, green methods using renewable materials are commonly used [55]. CQDs have been fabricated using hydrothermal processing and renewable resources such as aloe, fruit peel, silkworm, and paprika [56–59]. Despite the variety of available precursors, most synthesis techniques generally have serious drawbacks, such as high reaction temperatures, long preparation times, and low QY of CQD products. Fig. 6 shows the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs utilizing citric acid and 1,5-diaminonaphthalene (DAN) and the CQD structure [60]. The hydrothermal method is the most widely used technique for CQD synthesis due to its low cost, environmental friendliness, and ease of synthesis.

Microwave pyrolysis technology is one of the most popular bottom-up processes due to its ease and commercial feasibility. CQDs were fabricated using modified microwave irradiation, as shown in Fig. 7 [61]. Microwave irradiation interacts with opposing magnetic and electric forces within the solvent to heat it at the atomic level. This technology is effective because the high-frequency energy of electromagnetic radiation is transferred directly to the process without any physical interaction or intermediate processes [62]. This approach enabled faster reactions with higher selection yields and energies. Compared to conventional heating methods, the synthesis speed is fast, energy consumption is low, impurities are minimized, it is time-saving, environmentally friendly, provides temperature control, improved safety, scalability, and excellent reproducibility. A microwave pyrolysis approach to produce CQDs has been reported by mixing polyethylene glycol and sugar in water to form a clear solution and heating it with microwaves [63].

Ultrasonic synthesis technology is a technology that uses high-energy ultrasound to quickly create carbon nanomaterials under environmental conditions such as high pressure and temperature [64]. This technique combines factors such as morphology (size, shape, surface topography), solubility, chemical composition, and aggregation to determine the creation or modification of CQDs [65]. The change in CQDs is caused by the formation of an acoustic cavity and the subsequent sudden rupture of the liquid bubble, as seen in Fig. 8. Hydrodynamic cavitation occurs due to the large local pressure drop caused by the supersonic jet of liquid, resulting in severe foaming and further fragmentation of the macrocarbon material within the CQDs. Kumar et al. synthesized Sn@CQDs@Sn (hybrid anodes) using ultrasonic techniques [66]. CQDs synthesized by sonochemical technology are non-toxic compared to CQDs manufactured by other technologies and are excellent biocompatible materials for biomedical applications. To produce CQDs with desirable properties, sonication parameters such as power, frequency, and sonication time must be optimized. In ultrasonic technology, the PL properties and QY of CQDs can be improved by doping metals and other elements (anodes) [67]. CQDs manufactured by this technology are used in lotions, ointments, antibacterial agents, drug delivery, catalysts, superconducting devices, polymer synthesis, cell labeling, bioimaging, water purification, and energy storage devices due to their excellent physicochemical and fluorescence properties [68].

Heating in a suitable solvent system is an economical, environmentally friendly and easy way to make CQDs. Karak et al. [69] prepared CQDs using banana juice as a carbon source without specific doping reagents, and the resulting CQDs had an unusual size (spherical diameter of 3 nm) and a quantum yield of 8.9%. Va et al. [70] used a pseudo-stem heating method to synthesize CQDs with a size of 5 ± 2 nm and a quantum yield of 48%. Also, other carbon sources, such as rice bran [71] and mangosteen pulp extract [72], have been used to prepare CQDs using the solvothermal methods. Phadke et al. [73] synthesized CQDs from neem gum, where the CQDs had a size of 5–10 nm and were stable under various temperature conditions and pH. Zhao et al. [74] used corn husk to synthesize green CQDs using an inexpensive, simple, and convenient ethanol solvothermal method and found that the quantum yield and typical diameter of the synthesized green CQDs were 6.9% and 2.6 nm, respectively.

In addition to the methods described above, a simple and new method to synthesize CQDs is the pyrolysis method. Ye et al. [75] used a pyrolysis method to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) using biomass such as pigeon feathers, egg yolk, albumen, and manure, and the QY of the synthesized NS-CQDs was 24.87%, 16.34%, 17.48%, and 33.5%, respectively. Romero et al. [76] produced N- and S-doped CQDs photochemically using aqueous extracts of the plant and passivated with polyethylene glycol (PEG). They reported that with increasing UV irradiation period, the size of CQDs decreased, and the fluorescence intensity increased. The resulting CQDs acted as a reducing agent to reduce Ag+ and Au+ ions to their chemical states. Water-soluble CQDs with a size of 2.5 nm were produced through calcination using coconut as a raw material [11].

Other frequently used processes include hydrothermal carbonization, microwave irradiation, and carbonization. However, more efforts are needed to produce green CQDs using non-traditional (natural) resources rather than conventional heating. Microwave irradiation requires further research because it has a very fast reaction rate and disperses heat uniformly and efficiently. Size distribution often affects the fluorescence properties and toxicity of green CQDs. Fabricating green CQDs of uniform size is a significant challenge that hinders their use in biological applications. Different green CQD manufacturing processes affect their size, fluorescence color, QY, toxicity, and other properties [77].

The photoluminescence mechanisms of CQDs include (i) intrinsic luminescence associated with the size-dependent quantum confinement effect of the sp2-conjugated π-domain and recombination of localized electron-hole pairs, (ii) luminescence from surface states, including molecular and trap states. The molecular state is responsible for the fluorescence of CQDs derived from citrate, a carbon precursor [78]. The trap state-induced emission is associated with specific edge states on the sp3 carbon backbone and functional groups. The bandgaps of the σ and σ * states of the sp3 matrix have been shown to influence the π to π * electron levels of the sp2 domain [79,80]. The main advantages of CQDs over conventional organic phosphors are flicker-free FL and photostability [81]. Flash-free FL capability enables single molecule tracking, and photostability enables real-time imaging [82,83]. Experimental determinations rely on the controllable synthesis of well-defined CQDs, which are not yet fully developed. Because CQDs are very sensitive to many factors, it isn't easy to fully understand their FL characteristics [84]. CQDs with superior phosphorescence, longer visible emission periods, and more sensitive responses to small molecules can open an exciting frontier for biosensing. However, it is difficult to achieve long-term phosphorescence of CQDs at ambient temperature due to the lack of triplet excitons and nonradiative deactivation [85]. More interestingly, a new approach was developed using boric acid as a universal host matrix to activate long-lasting multicolor (blue, green, green-yellow, and orange) phosphorescence of CQDs at room temperature [86–89].

CQDs mainly contain carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen components, form multifunctional groups on the surface, and have excellent water solubility. The advantage of natural and chemical sources is that these methods, at best, require post-modification or separate reactants for surface passivation and doping. Proteins and other biomolecules impart self-passivation to CQDs. Carbon dots with apparent crystal lattice containing sp2/sp3 carbons [90]. All CQDs have chemical groups on their surface that play important roles, including nitrogen, oxygen, carboxyl, and amino groups. The π-π * transition of the CC bond makes the CQDs active in photon collection. The absorption peak at 220–280 nm is usually in the short wavelength range and assigned to the π-π * transition of the CC bond [91]. On the other hand, the shoulder peaks at 310–380 nm are assigned to n-π * transitions intersection. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of CQDs generally reveals an amorphous nature due to disordered carbon atoms, broad diffraction peaks with 2θ values ranging from 20 to 25°, and d-spacings from 0.31 to 0.38 nm. Carbon dots with different structures exhibit similar optical properties in phosphorescence, chemiluminescence, fluorescence, upconversion photoluminescence, electrochemiluminescence, and photoinduced electron transfer properties [92].

A well-known mechanism associated with surface oxidation and functional group levels is the release of surface states. The surface oxygen level of CQDs affects red-shifted emission [93]. As the oxygen content increases, surface defects become more numerous. According to our hypothesis, emission of surface states generates CQDs and the energy gap is determined by the size and surface properties of the CQDs. As a result, it was found that the electromagnetic field and surface oxidation had a significant impact on the surface state energy gap [94]. Higher levels of surface oxidation or electronic systems indicate lower energy gaps of CQD surface states. According to Huang's group, surface state emission and quantum confinement processes produce fluorescence in CQDs. The internal electron flow of each semiconductor nanocrystal partially crosses the crystal boundaries and exhibits bandgap and size-dependent energy relaxation modes.

Since carbon-based metal-free catalysts were first described as potential catalysts to replace conventional materials for oxygen reduction reactions (ORR), they have been considered as a form of potential low-cost material for environmentally friendly and viable energy conversion reactions. The function of a catalyst in a chemical process is called catalysis. Catalysis is a phenomenon in which a catalyst intervenes to speed up or slow down a chemical reaction. Catalysts can increase the rate of a reaction by chemically interacting with the reactants, altering the reaction pathway and lowering the activation energy of the reaction. The development of sophisticated CQD-based catalysts for clean and sustainable energy transition has received much attention over the past decade [95].

Several subcategories of pesticides include herbicides, molluscicides, plant growth regulators, fungicides, insecticides, bird/animal repellents, and rodenticides. Organophosphates, pyrethroids, organochlorines, and carbamates are some of the chemical pesticides found, according to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [96–98]. Glyphosate is a frequent herbicide that reduces weed development because of its relatively low toxicity [99,100]. According to the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) [100], excessive use is potentially carcinogenic to humans. USEPA has set a limit for contaminants in drinking water at 0.7 ppm [99,100]. Excessive use of triazolinone herbicides Amicarbazone [101] and Trifluralin [102], frequently used in corn, sugarcane, tree fruit and nut sectors, respectively, has also been linked to major health problems. In addition, eating these crops by animals and humans has caused serious health problems [103]. For a long time, pesticides have been used extensively to protect crops against weeds, pests and diseases, including both the original mineral types and modern chemically manufactured species [104]. It has become a global concern that the massive residues left behind by long-term overuse, overuse and misuse of pesticides can seriously endanger ecosystems and public health. Pesticides that do not decompose over time remain in crops and water and eventually redistribute spatially during natural cycles, accumulating over time [105,106]; then, soil [107] and water [108] are spatially redistributed [108,109]. Over time, consuming foods and related products with high pesticide residues can cause metabolic disorders, immune system problems, and other diseases [110–112]. Many people die each year, and there are clear health risks to workers exposed to excessive pesticide levels ( Table 1).

To some extent, pesticide accumulation accelerated species extinction and reduced the effectiveness of natural remedies [113]. Then, numerous countries and international organizations have implemented several assessments to strengthen pesticide regulation, including timely illegalizing and removing high-risk pesticides from the market and limiting the consumption of hazardous pesticides. It takes time to develop new natural pesticides that can replace other low-toxicity chemical pesticides, set the highest residue levels, and be sustainable and reasonably safe [114,115].
Adapted with permission from Issaka et al. [116]. Copyright Cell Press (2023).

In this context, optical sensing technology has attracted great interest in tracking chemical pesticides due to its intrinsic simplicity, good sensitivity, compact equipment, ease and economical, high throughput, and intuitive on-site detection [128]. The optical signal collected in these sensing platforms arises directly from the fluorescent material, which is important to ensure the high sensitivity of optical sensing systems for pesticides.

Additionally, the surface of the carbon core is enriched with various functional groups, including amino, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl, creating a framework for the functional use of CQDs. Due to these advantages, CQDs are increasingly used in various industrial fields, such as FL detection, optoelectronics, and environmental pollution monitoring [129,130]. Using FL CQDs as a signal indicator greatly improves detection performance, increasing its utility in pesticide detection.

Selective detection of metal ion analytes is essential for monitoring to minimize environmental contamination. Generally, oxygen surface functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in CQDs exist naturally and contribute to their hydrophilicity and high surface active area. Therefore, in addition to surface binding, metal ions can effectively coordinate with CQDs to tune their properties [131].

Even at low concentrations, mercury (Hg2+) ions are considered to be extremely hazardous to the food industry. Zhili et al. [132] developed an Hg2+ sensor with a sample recovery range of 102.0% to 103.0% and a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 3.3 nM using orange juice-derived N-CQDs. Radhakrishnan et al. [133] synthesized CQDs hydrothermally using Coccinia indica, and the CQDs showed great selectivity in the detection of Hg2+. According to the internal filter effect (IFE) and non-radiative electron transfer (ET) processes, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs weakens when Hg2+ is directly applied due to the interface between functional groups on the CQD surface and Hg2+ ions.

Lead (Pb2+), a toxic heavy metal, is attracting attention due to its negative effects on individuals, wildlife, and the environment. Amine-functionalized CQD-based sensor for selective detection of Pb2+ with a detection limit of 0.21 × 10-6 ‍μm was developed by Radhakrishnan et al. [133]. Due to electron transfer from the amine group in the excited state of CQDs to unoccupied d-orbitals of Pb2+, the addition of Pb2+ ions effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.

Cr(VI), one of the most dangerous metal ions, affects the immune system and is a harmful environmental pollutant. Roshni et al. [134] developed a sensitive Cr(VI) detection sensor with a detection limit of 0.1 ppm (1.9 μM) using CQDs prepared using peanuts. Chinawooth et al. [135] manufactured CQDs using shallot extract with an excellent quantum yield of 32.34% and a detection limit of 11.7 μM to detect the Cr(VI) ion created during the fluorescence quenching process. Showed a progressive decrease in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of Cr(VI) rose.

Copper, one of the most important and abundant ions, can be found in natural water and, in very high concentrations, can have long-term effects on the kidneys and liver and can also easily accumulate in our bodies. Murugan et al. [136] produced CQDs derived from Eleusine coracana, which were utilized as a "turn-off" sensor probe for Cu2+ detection with a linear range of 0–100 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 10 nM. To enable selective detection of Cu2+ ions with a LOD of 115 nM, a BPEI capping agent was applied to the CQDs [137] produced from bamboo leaves. The Cu2+ adsorption and BPEI desorption from CQDs surfaces were seen to be accompanied by a blue shift in photoluminescence quenching as the concentration of Cu2+ increased. The results of metal ion detection with CQDs are summarized in Table 2.

Zor et al. showed that increasing the number of heteroatom types (N, S, B) can change the emission wavelength of CQDs to match the absorption wavelength of the pesticide and provide better IFE [138]. If pesticides cannot directly affect the fluorescence of CQDs or have low selectivity, a linker or chemical reaction can be incorporated to suppress the fluorescence of CQDs to detect specific pesticides. Peng et al. employed pralidoxime to connect luminous magnesium and nitrogen-codoped CQDs (Mg, N-CQDs) with paraoxon [139]. Chlorpyrifos concentrations were as low as 3 ng/mL and were detectable using fluorescent CQDs coupled with Fe(II) and acetylcholine esterase/choline systems. Chlorpyrifos prevents the conversion of Fe(II) to Fe(III), which results in the increased fluorescence intensity of the probe system, whereas, in the absence of chlorpyrifos, Fe(III) quenches the fluorescence by coordinating with the oxygen atoms on the CQDs [133].

Similarly, N- and S-doped CQDs have been described for the highly sensitive detection of carbaryl, with a 5 µg/mL LOD, in which acetylcholinesterase and choline oxidase enzymes are utilized to produce H2O2. The fluorescence of the N/S-CQDs is then quenched by the H2O2 produced by the two-enzyme reaction from cholinergic chloride. The study demonstrates that N/S-CQDs give them a high H2O2 reactivity and sensitivity [140].

Li et al. [141] showed that organophosphorus pesticides can be detected by observing the dopamine polymerization process in the presence of acetylcholinesterase and acetylthiocholine as a substitute for enzyme-assisted H2O2-based response. Citric acid and thiourea were used to manufacture CQDs exhibiting red fluorescence at 610 nm. Dopamine polymerizes to create polydopamine, which has a fluorescence peak at 503 nm and uses a PET mechanism to quench the red-colored fluorescence of CQDs. Thiocholine, a by-product of the enzyme acetylcholinesterase, hindered the polymerization process in the presence of the enzyme by selectively adsorbing dopamine and maintaining the fluorescence of the CQDs. However, pesticides containing organophosphorus, such as paraoxon, can inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity. As a result, the polymerization product quenches the luminescent properties of CQDs. A reliable method for detecting pg/mL level is a ratiometric assessment of the luminescence of CQDs and polydopamine in the presence of paraoxon [142].

Yin Dai et al. [143] created a unique smartphone-enabled molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensing device for the targeted on-site detection of TMX. The response signal was generated by blue-emission CQDs encapsulated in a molecularly imprinted layer (B-CQDs@MIPs), whereas red-emission CQDs (R-CQDs) served as the internal reference. With increased TMX concentration, the sensor's fluorescence signal ratio rose, clearly changing the fluorescence color from red to blue. The sensor demonstrated a reasonable detection limit (LOD) of 13.5 nM in luminescence assessment and an LOD of 70.1 nM in visual determination. The sensing system was also tested using samples from the food and environment, showing recoveries ranging from 91.40% to 105.7%, indicating great dependability for TMX determination in real samples, as shown in Fig. 9.

Aptamers are used as sensors' biorecognition components to replace enzymes and increase selectivity. Short strands of DNA or RNA, called aptamers, are frequently used in many biosensors to detect biomarkers of infection and diagnose serious diseases. Aptamers are selected using SELEX (systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment) [144–147]. Pesticide-specific aptamers combined with CQDs have been described as a probe for recognizing the presence of acetamiprid to increase the sensitivity of pesticide sensors [148]. The cationic fluorescent CQDs made from cetrimonium bromide (CTAB) aggregate when the negatively charged aptamer binds to them, extinguishing their luminous properties. Due to the strong acetamiprid-aptamer specific binding, the complex of aptamer/CQDs disintegrates in the presence of acetamiprid, increasing the intensity of the fluorescence of the CQDs. The probe reportedly has a 0.3 nM acetamiprid detection limit. Table 3 summarizes the results of pesticide detection technology using modified CQDs.

In summary, the selectivity of CQDs-based probes for pesticide and metal ion detection depends on various variables, including the surface functional groups of the CQDs, the quenching and binding constants of the target pesticide, and stoichiometry. The functional group type of the targeted pesticide affects the interaction between the target pesticide and the CQDs and whether the interaction enhances or suppresses fluorescence. The sensitivity of CQDs-based probes is improved by using pesticide-specific linking materials and mediating electron transfer from CQDs to pesticides. It is possible to identify pesticides by tracking how they inhibit enzyme activity and how one of their metabolites, such as H2O2, affects the fluorescence properties of CQDs. It was found that molecularly imprinted polymers with pesticide-specific properties effectively improved the selectivity of CQDs for pesticides.

Biological contaminants are compounds unintentionally present in the environment, body fluids, and packaged foods and can pose health risks. These hazards can come from various sources, including food processing techniques, agricultural practices, and environmental pollutants. In this context, CQDs alone or in complex with appropriate active compounds can be used to build sensitive sensing platforms to detect desired biological contaminants in real samples. The following example summarizes the fabrication of green nanosensors based on CQDs and their derivatives for sensitively detecting numerous contaminants in biological matrices.

Shariati et al. developed CQDs and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP)-based green nanosensors to determine phenobarbital in plasma samples [159]. They prepared green fluorescent CQDs using a hydrothermal approach with cedrus as a carbon precursor and coupled them with MIP using 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane (APTES) as a functional monomer and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as cross-linker. The fabricated CQDs/MIP-based nanosensor exhibited excellent selectivity and detection ability toward phenobarbital in human blood plasma samples. They found that the performance of the fabricated nanosensor was excellent and reported a low detection limit of 0.1 nmol/L with a linear in the concentration range between 0.4 to 34.5 nmol/L.

In another study, a fluorescent nanosensor based on selective MIP and green fluorescent CQDs was developed by Sun et al. to determine the alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) in human serum samples [160]. The nanosensor was fabricated using V-CQDs (vinyl-functionalized CQDs) as support materials and transducer elements, AFP as the template protein, 4-vinylphenyl boronic acid (VPBA) and N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM) as the pH-responsive and thermo-responsive monomer, respectively, and N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA) and ammonium peroxodisulphate (APS) as the cross-linker and initiator, respectively. They found that the CQD/MIP-based fluorescent nanosensor achieved a low detection limit (0.474 ng/mL) and exhibited an excellent sensing response in the linear in the concentration range from 10 to 100 ng/mL. According to Jalili and Amjadi, glucose-derived CQDs impregnated with dopamine hydrochloride (CQD/MIP)-based nanosensor sensitively detected 3-nitrotyrosine in serum samples [161]. The basis for the sensitive detection of CQDs/MIP was the substantial quenching of CQDs/MIP fluorescence by 3-nitrotyrosine. The nanosensor also had a low detection limit (17 nM) and exhibited excellent linearity in the concentration range of 0.050–1.85 μM. They verified that the fabricated nanosensor exhibited high binding affinity and excellent selectivity for 3-nitrotyrosine and effectively recovered 3-nitrotyrosine from human serum samples.

Additionally, to identify biohazard factors in food samples, Liu et al. developed a green nanosensor using sweet potato peel-derived CQDs combined with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) to identify oxytetracycline (OTC) in honey samples [162]. They showed that the green fluorescent CQDs-based nanosensor possessed excellent sensing performance toward OTC with a relatively low detection limit (15.3 ng/mL) and recovery ranging from 90.2% to 97.3% in the honey sample. Zhang et al. investigated fast and sensitive detection of genistein in dried bean curd sticks, soymilk powder, soybean milk, and soya samples using N-doped CQDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine [163]. The CQDs-based nanosensor was efficiently employed to detect genistein with a low detection limit of 35.7 nM in soya-based products.

In another study, Wang et al. [164] detected domoic acid in lake water and shellfish samples using a nanosensor derived from CQDs coupled MIP. The CQDs were prepared hydrothermally using citric acid as the carbon source and coupled with MIP (utilizing tetraethoxysilane as a cross-linker and ammonia, 3-aminopropyl triethoxy silane as a functional monomer to develop CQD/MIP nanosensor. Additionally, they designed a paper-based sensor comprising CQDs/MIP nanoparticles and used it to quickly determine (20 min) the presence of domoic acid in real samples, improving the efficiency of on-site detection of domoic acid. With a low detection limit (10 nM), the developed sensor demonstrated exceptional stability and identification ability for domoic acid in lake water and shellfish samples. The sensor showed good linearity regarding reading responses ranging between 0.05 and 5 mM. To detect tetracycline in fish samples, Yang et al. developed a selective and sensitive nanosensor made of citric acid-derived CQDs [165]. Tetracycline was the target drug, and the constructed nanosensor effectively and accurately detected tetracycline in fish samples. Their study showed that the recovery range of tetracycline reached approximately 98.4% to 103.1% in fish samples with a detection limit of 9 mol/L.

Mycotoxins occur naturally in raw materials, feed, and food and accumulate in foods and other feed crops such as grains, peanuts, soybeans, sorghum, and corn in natural conditions. For example, adverse conditions, such as improper storage during the transport of harvested crops, can promote the growth of molds responsible for mycotoxin production [166]. In this regard, researchers developed various effective sensing tools for the sensitive determination of mycotoxins. For example, recently, Aggarwal et al. studied the ultrasensitive detection of citrinin and associated mycotoxins like ochratoxin A and aflatoxin B1 using cystamine-derived CQDs and Alizarin Red S (ARS) that aptly amalgamate with machine learning algorithms for automation [167]. The CQDs photoluminescence response as a function of different pH and solvents was employed to produce array channels that were further modulated in the mycotoxins presence whose digital pictures were taken to determine pixelation, essentially generating a barcode as shown in Fig. 10. Spiked milk, coffee, maize, gram, rice, and wheat samples were employed to assess the testing model. An exceptional accuracy of 100% was attained even at low mycotoxin concentration (10 pmol). Also, nanocomposites based on colloidal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticles (nanoceria) were reported to be highly efficient for the detection of ochratoxin A (a mycotoxin produced by Penicillium and Aspergillus strains) [168]. The significant spectral overlap and electrostatic interaction between the absorption spectrum of GQDs and the fluorescence emission spectrum of nanoceria-based aptasensors lead to an effective fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [169]. In occurring ochratoxin A (OTA)-specific aptamers, the addition of OTA halted the FRET process, resulting in significant fluorescence recovery with increasing OTA concentration. Under favorable experimental environments, the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor exhibited good selectivity toward OTA with a low detection limit (2.5 pg/mL) and following a substantial linear range (0.01–20 ng/mL). In addition, the GQD-based aptasensor efficiently determined the OTA in the real sample, like peanuts, with a recovery range between 90 to 110% [169].

In another study, Mao et al. developed a photoelectrochemical sensor based on citric acid-derived CQDs by utilizing the synergistic effect of heteroatom doping (sulfur and nitrogen) and copper phthalocyanine (CuPc) derivative non-covalent functionalization strategy [170]. The prepared photoelectrochemical sensor was used to detect OTA in OTA-contaminated rice. The results provided a validated low detection limit of 0.51 pg/mL with a wide linear range of 0.01–1000 ng/mL for OTA. The 97.33% and 102.0% recovery results also confirm that the CQD-based sensor possesses excellent OTA decision response in real-world applications. According to Li et al., a fluorescence quenchometric lateral flow immunochromatographic assay (FLFIA) based on CQDs conjugated to AgNP-Ab as acceptor signal probe and ovalbumin (OVA) as donor signal probe for detection of zearalenone (ZEN) was 10 times more accurate than the respective “turn-off” AgNP-based lateral flow immunochromatographic assay [171]. The detection limit of FLFIA for maize, wheat, vegetable oil, and vinegar samples ranged from 1 to 2.5 μg/kg. Based on the experimental results, they suggested that the FLFIA technique has great potential for rapid detection of ZEN in cereal samples. Despite the many successes in mycotoxin detection, future research is focused on developing promising nanomaterials with outstanding optical or electrochemical properties that are affordable, simple to manufacture, and ecologically benign.

Kanamycin (KAN), an aminoglycoside antibiotic secreted mostly by Streptomyces, can kill bacteria by attaching to ribosomes and interfering with protein synthesis. To prevent the risk of food safety hazards caused by KAN, China National Standard GB 3165–2019 and European Union Council Regulation 37/2010/EC stipulate the maximum residue limit of KAN in foods of animal origin (excluding fish) as 150 μg/kg milk, 2500 μg/kg kidney, 600 μg/kg liver, 100 μg/kg fat, and 100 μg/kg muscle [172]. There is growing concern about using nanoparticle-based sensors for rapid and accurate detection of KAN in food. We constructed a fluorescent label-free aptasensor for determining KAN using an internal filtering effect (IFE) based on citrate-derived CQDs and AuNPs [173]. In the presence of KAN, binding of the aptamer to the target prevents it from attaching to the AuNPs, allowing the AuNPs to aggregate at high salt concentrations without interfering with the fluorescence of the CQDs. The proposed aptasensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 18 nM and can detect KAN in the linear range of 0.04 to 0.24 μM under optimal conditions. In addition, the applicability of the proposed aptasensor in milk samples with a recovery range of 90.0–98.3% indicates its practicality for future perspectives on the detection of KAN in foods. In another report, a fluorescent aptasensor based on graphene oxide quantum dots (GO-CQDs) containing single-stranded DNA binding protein was used for sensitive detection of KAN in milk samples [174]. The GO-CQD-based aptasensor with high stability, selectivity, and anti-interference features exhibited a low detection limit (6 pg/mL) and an excellent linear range of 0.01–90 ng/mL for KAN determination.

Additionally, Zhao et al. reported “off” and “off” assays based on cholesterol- and o-phenylenediamine-derived yellow fluorescent CQDs for the sensitive detection of KAN and adenosine triphosphate [175]. They found that a “turn-off-on” mode was applied to detect KAN with a detection limit of 7.2 μM by hydrogen bond formation, and a “turn-off-on” mode was applied to determine adenosine triphosphate (detection limit of 93 nM). In another report, Wang et al. developed a surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy sensor using two distinct signal amplification approaches, namely a Zn-CQDs cascade oxidation-reduction reaction switch controlled by a nucleic acid aptamer and the surface plasmon resonance of gold nanorods for the trace detection of KAN in food [176]. The Zn-CQDs sensor with a low detection limit of 3.03 × 10−13 g/mL provided a linear response ranging from 10−12 to 10−5 g/mL for determining KAN. Also, honey and milk powder samples spiked with KAN were analyzed, with recoveries ranging from 107.2% and 84.1%, demonstrating the potential of CQDs-based sensors for determining the risk factors in food products. Moreover, the nonspecific KAN adsorption on nanomaterials was often overlooked, leading to an incorrect interpretation of some aptasensor concepts. Therefore, it is crucial to establish strategies to limit nonspecific adsorption of the target to advance the development of aptasensors for KAN detection using CQDs [177–179].

Chloramphenicol is a cost-effective and potent antibiotic commonly used to treat sick animals. However, accumulation of Chloramphenicol in the human body can cause hematological toxicity and permanent aplastic anemia. Regulations are currently in effect in several countries prohibiting the use or presence of Chloramphenicol in foods for human consumption. Several analytical techniques or nanotechnology sensors have been evaluated to quantify and detect Chloramphenicol to protect consumers [180]. Among these analytical strategies, optical and electrochemical sensors offer many advantages for detecting traces of Chloramphenicol, including fast analysis speed, simple operation, and high sensitivity. For example, nitrogen and phosphorus doping in arginine-derived CQDs (N, P-CQDs) developed a sensitive fluorescent probe to determine the Chloramphenicol in milk samples [181,182]. Doping CQDs with heteroatoms regulate the electronic energy level structures and improve the fluorescent performance of N, P-CQDs. Notably, the N, P-CQDs were used to detect Chloramphenicol in milk samples highly selectively and sensitively, with recovery rates ranging from 95.60 to 104.32%. In addition, a detection limit of Chloramphenicol was found to be 0.36 mM, with a linear range of 0.8 to 70 mM.

Further, Liu et al. reported a CQD/MIP-based photochemical sensor developed by sol-gel polymerization of citric acid-derived CQDs as fluorescent sources and functional monomers without the addition of and extra functional monomer [183]. The photochemical sensor could determine chloramphenicol in the linear range between 5–100 μg/L with 1 μg/L of detection limit. The findings suggested that CQDs-based fluorescent molecular imprinting sensors effectively determined chloramphenicol in milk with satisfied recoveries ranging from 99.01–108.80%. Bu et al. developed an electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted polypyrrole at citric acid-derived CQDs and Zr-MOF-modified electrodes for the supersensitive detection of chloramphenicol in tap water [184]. They found that the sensor exhibited a low detection limit of 0.061 pmol/L with a good linearity range of 0.1–100 pmol/L for chloramphenicol. The recoveries of chloramphenicol in tap water were recorded to be 96.0–103.6%, unveiling high specificity and sensitivity for the trace detection of chloramphenicol. Recently, a selective and sensitive electrochemical sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene CQDs (NGDYQDs) was reported to quantify chloramphenicol in water samples [185]. The basic concept of this quantification is based on the strong electrocatalytic activity of NGDYQD, which reduces the -NO2 group to the hydroxylamine group of the chloramphenicol moiety. The prepared sensor had a detection limit of approximately 5 nM, a sensitivity of approximately 8.79 μA−1 μM−1 cm−2, a linear response of 0.1–114.5 μM, and exhibited high stability, reproducibility, and repeatability. Despite significant advances in optical and electrochemical sensing probes over the past decade, they continue to face many challenges, such as a small range of materials that are active and stable enough for chloramphenicol analyses, problems with reproducibility in sensor fabrication, and problems with material falling off the electrode during analysis. Further investigation is needed to improve electrochemical and optical sensor technologies for chloramphenicol and other analytes. To improve system design, it is first necessary to understand the operating mechanism of the sensor.

Antibiotics are widely applied in both human and veterinary medicine to combat bacterial infections. Antibiotics such as tetracycline (TC) are frequently used in agriculture and are inexpensive and effective. However, antibiotic residues have been detected in staple foods, vegetables, dairy products and freshwater sources, posing a serious risk to human well-being. Ingestion of these antibiotic residues can lead to the development of multi-drug resistant (MDR) microbial strains, leading to adverse health outcomes such as nephrotoxicity and intolerance. Therefore, it is important to detect antibiotic residues in meat, dairy and other foods. Recently, various methods such as colorimetric methods, fluorescence methods, electrochemical methods, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and surface-enhanced Raman scattering methods have been widely used to detect antibiotics [186–189].

Nonetheless, these techniques require time-consuming sample preparation steps and require advanced equipment. Additionally, semiconductor quantum dots, metal-organic frameworks, gold nanoclusters, and other inorganic fluorophores with stable and strong emission properties have been used for antibiotic detection. Nevertheless, it has certain limitations, including low water solubility, heavy metal contamination, and, in some cases, fading of organic dyes. As a result, recent research interest has shifted to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) due to their outstanding properties.

The presence and identification of antibiotics depend on the fluorescence properties of CQDs and how they react when bound to antibiotics. Detection of specific antibiotics was achieved by analyzing the unique fluorescence patterns resulting from their interaction with CQDs [190]. Antibiotic interaction with CQDs can enhance or decrease the fluorescence signal [191]. Qian and colleagues employed near-infrared emitting CQDs to detect TC and quinolones [192]. In the presence of these antibiotics, the fluorescence of near-infrared CQDs was significantly reduced due to a phenomenon known as the internal filter effect (IFE). This reduction in CQDs fluorescence allows the quantification of antibiotics with detection limits of 0.5 nM for oxytetracycline and 6.3 nM for norfloxacin.

On the other hand, when aptamer-modified gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) are introduced into the NaCl medium, they quench the fluorescence of CQDs through IFE. However, in the presence of kanamycin, the fluorescence of CQDs is restored due to the specific interaction between the aptamer and kanamycin. This interaction leads to the aggregation of Au NPs [193].

Introducing heteroatoms into carbon quantum dots (CQDs) increases the active site, allowing easy customization of the properties and reactivity of CQDs for improved antibiotic detection [194–197]. For example, Niu and Gao performed the pyrolysis of glutamic acid, forming N-doped CQDs (NCQDs) without external doping agents [198]. The decomposition of glutamic acid produced carbon- and nitrogen-based residues, concurrently constructing NCQDs. These NCQDs, averaging 4.6 nm in size, exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence and find application in detecting the widely used antibacterial drug amoxicillin. In the presence of amoxicillin, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs undergoes enhancement and broadening, accompanied by a significant blue shift. This change is attributed to reduced non-radiative transitions due to amoxicillin adsorption on the NCQDs' surface, diminishing energy transfer between the NCQDs. The drug-binding on NCQDs also reduces surface defects, further amplifying fluorescence intensity [198]. When metal ions are present, adding antibiotics to CQDs may lead to varying degrees of fluorescence quenching or fluorescence recovery. This effect serves as a transduction mechanism for antibiotic detection [199].

The CQDs generated from citric acid and formamide (size ∼2.8 nm) can detect oxytetracycline, with a detection limit of 60 nM. These CQDs possess abundant NH2 and COOH groups on their surface, forming chelating complexes with metal ions (Cu2+, Ce3+, and Eu3+). However, in the presence of antibiotics containing OH, CO, and COOH groups, competition arises for interaction with the metal ions to form complexes. This competition significantly impacts the fluorescence of CQDs and has proven effective for antibiotic detection in shrimp and fish samples. Furthermore, CQDs prepared from citric acid and urea, modified with piperazine, have been used to detect oxytetracycline. These fluorescent CQDs exhibit a notable redshift upon binding with oxytetracycline, attributed to Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) between the CQDs and oxytetracycline. Enhancement in fluorescence intensity has been observed, contingent on pH and oxytetracycline concentration. However, no emission peak shift occurred with increasing oxytetracycline concentration, suggesting that the fluorescence arises from the interaction between oxytetracycline and piperazine on the CQD surface [200].

Various CQD types have been used to detect tetracycline utilizing the internal filter effect (IFE) [201]. A CQDs-based sensing array featuring both dual and quadruple fluorescence channels has been designed to detect and differentiate multiple tetracyclines like chlortetracycline, oxytetracycline, tetracycline, and doxycycline [202,203]. Blue-emitting CQDs (B-CQDs, ∼3 nm) synthesized via a hydrothermal process using citric acid and ethylenediamine, and green-emitting CQDs (G-CQDs, ∼16 nm) produced via microwave synthesis from citric acid and urea, have demonstrated effectiveness as sensing elements. In the presence of tetracycline, fluorescence quenching occurs in CQDs due to IFE without a shift in the emission wavelength. Each tetracycline exhibits unique changes in fluorescence intensity, allowing differentiation of different tetracyclines. However, in a two-channel array consisting of only G-CQD and B-CQD, the four tetracyclines cannot be completely distinguished. Combining two CQDs (m-CQD) exhibits the same absorption band as individual CQDs but with an enhanced value due to the sum of the absorption values of B-CQD and G-CQD. This approach allows the differentiation of individual tetracyclines. Chlortetracycline can be detected up to 3.30 nM using CQDs prepared through the hydrothermal method of p-phenylenediamine and acetic acid and emit orange-red fluorescence [204]. Upon interaction with chlortetracycline, CQDs show enhanced fluorescence with a blue shift in ethanol medium, probably due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). However, in the presence of water, quenching due to depolymerization causes fluorescence quenching, which is proportional to the water concentration. The detection limit for chlortetracycline in this scenario was 32.9 mM. As a result, in addition to chlortetracycline detection, these CQDs can also be used to measure the water content of organic solvents, which are important in chemical reactions. Additionally, europium-doped CQDs (Eu-CQDs) with strong stability and blue fluorescence are effective in selectively detecting tetracycline in water samples, achieving a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.3 mM. In the presence of tetracycline, the fluorescence of Eu-CQDs is quenched due to the internal filter effect (IFE) between tetracycline and Eu-CQDs [205].

The fluorescence activation mechanism is another approach used for antibiotic detection. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) produced through hydrothermal treatment with p-dihydroxybenzene and hydrazine hydrate were utilized for ampicillin detection. In the presence of Fe3+ ions, the blue fluorescence of CQDs is selectively quenched due to metal-ligand coordination and electrostatic interactions between the functional groups of CQDs and Fe3+. However, when ampicillin is introduced under 365 nm UV irradiation, the non-fluorescent CQDs-Fe3+ complex exhibits a selective fluorescence increase, achieving a detection limit of 0.70 mM for ampicillin. These CQDs can also be employed for tetracycline detection, as the CQDs' fluorescence quenching occurs through peptide interaction and electron transfer. A ratiometric sensing approach has been used to enhance sensor reliability, and CQDs with dual emission properties have been developed for penicillin G detection in milk samples [206]. Yellow emissive CQDs with molecularly imprinted receptors interact with penicillin G, leading to quenching of the yellow emission due to analyte blocking, while the blue emission remains constant, enabling a ratiometric readout.

In addition to CQDs produced from different chemicals, CQDs derived from natural precursors have also shown excellent stability and efficacy in detecting harmful antibiotics [207–209]. For example, Wang et al. demonstrated a specific identification of amoxicillin utilizing hydrothermally produced CQDs (4–8 nm) from beet. These CQDs demonstrate excitation-dependent emission with a redshift from 438 to 456 nm as the excitation wavelength shifts from 340 to 400 nm [207]. The fluorescence of CQDs is significantly enhanced in the presence of amoxicillin, with minimal variations in the presence of other coexisting compounds. Similarly, CQDs prepared from beet with a size of 6 nm have been applied for norfloxacin detection [208]. These stable CQDs, with excitation-dependent fluorescence, exhibit enhanced fluorescence when bound with norfloxacin, achieving a lower detection limit (LOD) of 0.937 mM. Nitrogen-doped CQDs (NCQDs) produced from durian shell using Tris base as a doping agent via a simple hydrothermal treatment have shown high selectivity for tetracycline detection, with a LOD of 75 nM [209]. These spherical NCQDs, approximately 6.5 nm in size, are fluorescent (blue), stable over a wide pH range (3−11), and resistant to photobleaching, although affected by temperature. In the presence of tetracycline, fluorescence quenching of NCQDs occurs due to the internal filter effect. Also, Guo et al. reported the detection of tetracycline using fluorescent CQDs obtained through pyrolysis of crab shells [210]. Highly stable CQDs with an average size of 10 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of 30% can detect tetracycline with an LOD of 5 mg/L. Table 4 shows a comparison of previously reported CQD-based probes for antibiotic detection.

In summary, fluorescent CQDs from various sources effectively detected antibiotics in a variety of food materials. Antibiotic detection strategies rely on relatively simple fluorescence “turning on” or “turning off” mechanisms, but the selectivity of CQDs for specific antibiotics limits their applications. Additional studies are needed to explore the selectivity of CQD-based antibiotic detection for antibiotics with similar structural properties. Additionally, other fluorescence quenchers in the sample may affect the results, so developing efficient antibiotic sensors requires a thorough investigation of the unique interactions between CQDs and antibiotics.

A thorough mechanistic study is essential for CQD-based fluorescence detection of food samples. Additionally, more robust methods must be discovered to increase the fluorescence quantum yield. The ability to use inexpensive carbon precursors and electrodes, mild reaction conditions, simple equipment, and good product yields are key advantages of the electrochemical synthesis of CQDs. The resulting CQDs are of excellent quality. In some cases, the particle size can be altered by adjusting the applied potential, and they exhibit excellent fluorescence properties without the need for laborious passivation and purification techniques. Low cost, good sensitivity, simple measurement and analysis, good photochemical stability and economic advantages. The use of electrodes, temperature constraints, mass transfer limitations, and dependence on photoelectrode material quality mainly reduce sensitivity [211]. The advantages and disadvantages of CQD-based sensors are summarized in Table 5.

As an essential component of the carbon family, CQDs are naturally endowed with high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and durability of carbon-based materials. Additionally, CQDs have been shown to possess unique properties of excellent fluorescence, high biocompatibility, minimal cytotoxicity, and simple production. Combined with the unexpected optical and biological properties inherent in carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs are attracting increasing attention, especially in the field of detection of food contaminants. The application of CQD to identify food contaminants, including organic contaminants, biological agents, and antibiotics, appears promising. However, CQDs-based detection systems have some important drawbacks, and further research is needed to address these issues.
(i)
Sustainability considerations (e.g., environmental pollution, toxicity, recyclability and reusability) must be taken into account when creating new synthetic technologies and materials. More work is needed to generate the green precursors needed to synthesize CQDs.
(ii)
Future research is needed from a detection perspective to detect multiple pollutants and eradicate interference from various pollutants simultaneously. Integrating the benefits of many CQDs-based technologies is one option. Additionally, more research is needed on CQD detection with low detection limits.
(iii)
Depending on the application, complex food matrices must undergo several pretreatment procedures before detection. As a result, it is not easy to perform direct detection without preprocessing, and future studies are needed to optimize or omit preprocessing procedures to facilitate the practical use of these QD-based approaches.
(iv)
Although much research has been performed to integrate CQDs into portable materials, more work needs to be done to maintain the stability, sensitivity, and ease of storage of mobile sensors.
(v)
The goal of CQD-based sensors is to create highly effective nanosensors with more specialized sensitivity and improved portability, stability, and reproducibility.

Despite their limited use, CQDs have many remarkable properties that make them potentially useful nanomaterials for food analysis. With the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, the development of more advanced CQDs and CQD-based composites is expected to greatly improve the practicality of these materials in the detection and analysis of food contaminants. Thus, CQDs can better overcome the interference of irrelevant elements and improve the selectivity of the detection procedure, providing new environmental observation and food safety detection technology.

Rhim Jong-Whan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization. Kumar Dr. Jothi Vinoth: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization.
